speaker: interviewee
text: With Michael Librach in Scarsdale, New York. Today is November 2nd, 1995, and I'm Terry Tull. Michael, you just described a pretty harrowing journey out of Lodz to France. It's January, 1940, and you have just entered the Polish Army in exile--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in France, which is a part of the French Army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first see action?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the front about April-- April, '40-- '40. In April '40, we went covering their [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] space in between the Maginot Line, and we were first of our division to cover. We had been covering a Senegalese infantry in front of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you know, this was, like, mid-April, and the Germans, when they started the push, which was beginning of-- end of May, I think, or something like that, they started the big push just to overrun France, and that's where they came through.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we were there, the Senegalese run away, and we were shooting at the Germans-- German infantry with 75-millimeter guns, which are very unwieldy for this kind of fighting. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where in France was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mostly towards-- very close to Alsace-Lorraine. But it was not directly in Alsace-Lorraine. We were a little west of it. Yeah. Finally, one day we found out that we are completely surrounded. And our officers told us to just drop the arms and try to penetrate through the lines into southern France. The French army would be-- start reconstructing itself there. And so we were walking very stupidly on a ridge, on top of a ridge, and there was a French soldier in front of us screaming [FRENCH]. Here, come here. And we thought that he's just directing us how to get through. And we came to him and two, three bushes opened behind him and the Germans are were there with machine guns. So, you know, there was absolutely no way that we could prevent being captured.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this specific location? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Saint Die. Yes. It's a small town called Saint Die. It was in that area. And I don't think it was in town. It was outside of town, so Saint Die, in France. And I don't remember exactly, but in the same area, the German placed us in some kind of a temporary camp. And they were so disorganized, they took the whole French Army prisoner and-- at the same time, about two million people-- that the food was not there. There was no food. For six weeks we lived there, eating grass and so on. So this was really hunger time. And then from there, we went and we were sent to the first stalag that I was in, which was a main camp where, of course, prisoners of war were being placed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain the word stalag? What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's a Stammlager, which means a main camp, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] abbreviated as stalag.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the number of stalag you were at?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one was 7A. I think it was 7A. I'm not very sure. It was-- the first one was the in Bavaria somewhere, and I don't know exactly where it was, the town. And when we got there, that was the first time that some kind of data was taken from us-- names and so on. When we came into camp, the camp was full of Polish prisoners of war who were taken in the Polish Campaign, 1939. So that when that the time came to go and give them the data and fill all these forms, they were sitting at tables and they were the ones who were in the offices, under supervision of Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I came up on a young non-com of the Polish Army, sitting there and writing. And he wrote out my name, and my age, and so on, and the languages I spoke, which was, at that time, German, and French, and Polish. And then he came and asked me about religion, and I said I'm Jewish. And he looks at me and says, do you want to put that down? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now what went through my mind? What will happen to-- to my people? What will happen to me? And secondly, what is my race if, you know, if I'm pointed out, as I-- I have to be? Being with Poles, who are basically very antisemitic, or, you know, some of them are, even not all them. I know that I'll be pointed out. This is a Jew. So what will happen then? So I said, yes, put it down. At this point, a non-commissioned German officer comes by and says, oh, this man knows languages. We need him in the office, which would have been a soft job, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he takes my card and goes up front. There is another non-com, German non-com, who checks these cards, says, I can't give you this man. This is-- this is a Jew. Das ist ein Jude. And he stamped my card, 'Jude,' Jew, across the card. OK. So I have this-- this on my card, and we go back into the camp, and all of a sudden, I'm being called to the office of the Kommandant. And he says to me-- he says to me, Librach, you are the only Jew in the camp there-- in that barrack. I want to know everything that goes on this barrack, or the things will be very rough for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said-- I didn't say anything. I just walked out. Among the Polish prisoners of war, I had some-- a friend who worked for my family in the factory, a worker. And he already knew the-- the things what is going in the camp. And I say, what do I do now? And he says, are you going-- are you-- do you know whether you're going out on-- on a work detail or whatever? I said, yeah, we're supposed to leave tomorrow. He says, thank God because they would be very rough with you. So the next day, I was out. I was out. We went to-- with a large group of Polish prisoners of war to the town of Traunstein, in the Ba-- Bavarian Alps, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which is a beautiful, beautiful scenery.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's close to Berchtesgaden, which was the place where Hitler built his famous home, and to work on a railroad track. And that's where I spent, oh, close to a year, went through a winter. Things are pretty rough because, you know, you worked all day in the snow, especially during the winter-- wet and cold and so on. You had to go out again the next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe an average day? What time would you wake up? Did you have breakfast? How long did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had breakfast, but I have no really-- real memory of what we were eating and how we were eating. At that time, we were still purely on the-- on what the Germans gave us. Later on, as you'll find out, we already had some ways of getting our own food and so on, but that was in different camps. In this camp, the work was pretty hard. Although, at the very beginning, we went to do some work in agriculture, in farms and so on, and I remember trying to get some hay on a fork. And, you know, this kid who was playing in nightclubs and so on, never worked with his hands, and that wasn't very easy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had to learn that. I had to learn to work physically. And what happened was that I was one of the hardest workers because, if you finished this kind of work up to this point, if you get to this point in your work, digging a ditch, for example, you can sit down, and that's your day. You know, the Germans would assign you certain amount of work. So that was-- that was pretty hard to-- to get into that kind of work, especially that I was with a group of Polish peasants. Because who was pulled into the army were Polish peasants who went to-- to do agriculture work in France. These were the-- these were the friends that I had-- that I fought with. And, you know, they-- to them, this kind of physical work was-- was nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, it was-- believe me, it was something at the beginning. I remember the first day when I couldn't get the hay on my fork, so they sent me up into the barn to just walk on hay, to-- to-- to make it-- you know, to pat it down. And that was my work for the day. Pretty tiring, but, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people who were with me there, other prisoners, were rather nice to me. You know, there was myself and another Jewish boy from Paris, another Jewish man. We have a picture from there, from Traunstein, where I have a picture with this other Jewish boy. Someday-- one day, a German officer of the guard came to me and says, you know, my family has never seen a Jew. Could you two guys come in and I'll take a picture of you? And he did. So we were then representative of the Jewish race for them. And-- So in this environment of-- of camps for prisoners of war, you didn't see brutality or beatings, although there was always a threat that if you didn't behave or something, some officer--

speaker: interviewee
text: There might been a threat, but you have to understand the basic thing about my story is that we were under the Wehrmacht. We were under the normal army forces. The camps-- the concentration camps-- were under the SS. There's a big difference. That's one point. The other point is-- there are two more points in that. One more point is that the Germans have an absolute fetish of uniforms. They worship uniforms, no matter what uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even if you're the enemy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Even if you're the enemy, you have a uniform. And the third one was Geneva Convention, which was observed. We screamed Geneva Convention anytime something happened to us, and they observed the Geneva Convention. The German Army. It has to be understood that I am talking about the army. I'm not talking about the Waffen-SS, or the SR, or any of the Hitler part, which were also fighting. But we belonged to the Wehrmact. We belonged to the army of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: As prisoners of war, did you wear prison-type uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were in our own uniforms. We were in our uniforms, and when they got torn or something, the Germans have captured enough stores of French Army to give a-- give us another pair of pants or another jacket, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And even though you were one or just a few Jews in this group, mostly your comrades were friendly to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have to say that they were. I don't know what happened later on when I was picked out to go the Jewish camp, whether somebody pointed me out or not. But I did not feel antisemitism coming from the people I was with. I would-- just to-- you know, in Traunstein, what we were doing, my-- our basic work after this agriculture thing-- our basic work that we were doing there was maintaining the-- the railroad tracks, which is hard work, you know, laying tracks and so on. And there was an old railroad man who was directing that work. He wasn't guarding us, but he was telling us what to do. And this man, every evening, would get on his bicycle and go to all the pubs in the area and pick up all the cigarette stubs, go home, empty them, and in the morning, he would give every one of us a pinch of tobacco. And at that point, we didn't have anything to smoke. We were all smokers, and it was-- I think that we have to talk about the things that we have-- I-- that I  have encountered which were kindness-- which were kindness. Not everything was-- was terrible, and not everything was brutality. But there was also kindness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he take a risk in-- in doing this every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know whether he-- yes, probably if some crazy Hitler follower would know about it. You have to understand that we were in Bavaria, a strongly Catholic country. They were not for Hitler. Hitler persecuted Catholics also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you know, that has a big part of it, that in Bavaria probably things like this could happen much more often than anywhere else. But when we deal with people, one-to-one, it's a very big difference. Antisemitism is a group feeling. It's a feeling, Jews are terrible. But I-- the one Jew I know, oh, I love the guy, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the type of things that was happening. On a one-to-one basis, he didn't care that we were Jewish, this old man. As a matter of fact, most of them-- us-- weren't. I was the-- I was one of the two Jews there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is what-- I think you'll see in my-- in my whole story that there were a few, few cases like that where I found something that was unusual, and unusual as a kindness. Could you describe the physical location of the plant-- excuse me, the physical location of the camp? Did you have barbed wire? Did you have fences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not on work commandos, so called, Arbeitskommandos. Yes, the Krems an der Donau, the stalags not only had-- they barbed wires, but they had towers with-- with machine guns and so on. But no, the working con-- working groups were-- were billeted in-- in homes. You know, some of the prisoners, not-- not our-- the groups that I was with were even billeted in private homes. You know, like one man would work the field for one peasant. Sometimes the man was away at war, and he was there with the woman of the house, and this was prisoner of war. And at this point, I was still with the general group of prisoners of war. I was not with the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: You yourself, did you have any opportunity to come in contact with civilians in the surrounding area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen? Could you give an example.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now I, at that point, it wouldn't happen very often, except, for example, for a railroad man who would pass by and who would give us a pinch and you could say hello and goodbye to him. But the next-- the next type of work that I was doing, the next camp, we-- we-- I working in a-- in a barn, with-- taking care of cows for a whole winter. And I had to deal with the priests there because that were church goods-- the priest, and the people who work for the priest, and the other people, the civilians who were taking care of the land, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was just one of the workers there, talking to them, eating at the same table. We were eating there. We had to go to sleep in our little camp where they put us in some big barn or something, not in anybody's home. But we ate-- the-- the few of us who were assigned to that particular farm-- this was a church farm-- we ate at the table with the workers there. So this is the next camp. After-- after the Traunstein, which was pretty hard, but was all right, I-- I started working pretty well as the physical worker. So one day, all of a sudden, they transfer us to Austria, Krems an der Donau, which was the other 17B, I think-- Stalag 17B-- and send us to work in Herzogenburg, Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: Herzogenburg was a small town, and I was still with the Polish prisoners of war there. And as I said, I worked for this farm of the-- of the church-- of the Catholic Church-- both in the field, and the second year, I was in-- during the winter, I was in the barn with the cows, which was warm and nice-- nice job, very nice job. We ate very well there because they were fed in the farm. And time was going on. There was nothing very unusual happening there until-- until one day, a German non-com in, called my name, and took me with him. And took me with him to a camp that was specifically for Jewish prisoners of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know why he did this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they were-- they were looking for Jews all over, in every camp, in every Arbeitskommando. They were picking Jews out and taking to this camp. They were putting Jews together. We didn't bide very good. It wasn't a very good news because we knew that if they have us together, someday we might die together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] However, one thing is very significant. You know, we knew what was going on. We knew that we'll be treated much worse than being with our comrades in arms, the Polish, French, or whatever. And yet, every single Jewish prisoner that was brought into that barrack would say, thank God, I'm-- I'm on my own. Thank God I don't have to hide anymore. Because some of them were-- you know, have not reported that they are Jewish. Some of them have hidden their ident-- Jewish identity. They were pointed out or whatever. And they were hiding, you know, as a Jew. And they were all saying, I don't have to hide anymore. What happens, happens. And this was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- did you feel that way too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I lived as a Jew. I never-- I was never in hiding. I never hidden my identity, even to the Germans there. They knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you just said before that there was-- there was a danger in that, if all of you Jews are put together, you might have a much worse situation than if you're mixed in with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but there was nothing I could do about it. Of course I could-- you know, some people think that it's a-- it's a duty of a prisoner of war to escape and fight on. It was impossible. It was impossible. And in my case, it was even-- it would have been much worse because if I went to Poland, everybody knew me and knew me as a Jew, I would have been taken to a concentration camp right away. If I went to France, I didn't know anybody. I had absolutely no support behind me. So escaping was not-- not a-- not a possibility for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever here of anyone who escaped from a POW camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not-- not Jews, yes, others. There was a possibility for people who were close to the Swiss border. There were people who escaped and-- and reached Switzerland. But most of them, of the people who attempted escape, were brought back to camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So now we are here in Herzogenburg and working in good-- pretty good conditions, and I am taken into that different camp. And, you know, I have no idea what it will be, and I have no idea what kind of work it will be, what kind of food I can provide for myself, and so on. Because we were-- already, the food the Germans were giving us was very, very bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: But right away, I became a driver of two horses. And firstly, I want to describe-- the camp itself was divided into several parts. One part was the Jews. Another part was Russians-- who did not have Geneva Convention protection-- and they were basically there-- they were being sent back to-- back to-- directly into concentration camps, because they were not protected. So-- especially the Jews. The Jews in Russian Army were picked out from among the Russian prisoners of war. They were sent. And there was also, very well guarded, separated from us, Americans and-- and British. Now and I want to go back to Krems an der Donau and tell you that while they could separate the French and the Poles and the Yugoslavs, I have seen a company of British soldiers come in, into the camp, at military pace, in line, beautifully. And then they stop in front of the Kommandant's office, and the Kommandant comes out and says, Jews, three steps forward, and the whole company stepped forward. They could not separate them because then the others didn't go for it. That was British.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pretty amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this pretty amazing. That happened, of course, back in my story. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Krems. I have seen that in Krems, which when we got into Austria. And that, you know, probably happened all over Germany several times, but that's the one I have seen.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. In this camp for Jewish POWs-- or, as it turns out, there were several different kinds of POWs segregated but in the same camp-- do you remember where this camp was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the corner where Austria, Czechoslovakia, and Germany-- the prewar borders-- meet, in that area. There was about 50 villages that were evacuated, and this was a tremendous training camp for the German army. I had-- have been assigned two horses and a wagon, and I was transporting things. My job was to clean the horses in the morning, hook them up to-- to the-- to the wagon, and then just drive them around. And that allowed me, and people like me, to do two things. First, deal with the German and-- and the forced labor population for food. I have to mention that at this point already, I established a connection which my father in-- in America, and I was getting cigarettes sent from Canada by thousands. And that was a currency in Germany. So I could buy a piece of lard or a piece of-- or-- or a chicken or something like that. And my best friend in camp, who was a tailor from Strasburg, Abe Gross, was a very good cook, so that we cooked for ourselves. We cooked the things that I could get and so on. So I was in rather privileged position, because I-- I had this freedom of being around, and I was being loaded by either German political prisoners or by Russians, loaded and unloaded.

speaker: interviewer
text: This camp, were there barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were barracks, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived in a barracks--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: With this friend from Strasburg, and you had kitchen facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were-- the room was for about 20-- 20 men. It was bunks, the same you've seen from Auschwitz, the same type of bunks, only we had-- we had so much better life. We cannot compare our suffering to theirs. We were deprived of liberty, but they were deprived of life, so.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that there were Jew-- Jewish soldiers from all over Europe in this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Yugos-- Yugoslav, French, and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: French. French. Yugoslavs, of course, didn't understand. Some of them did because some of the people from Belgrade, from capital of Yugoslavia, the Jews from capital all spoke French. But French was our language inside the camp. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What information were you able to get about what was going on in the war, or what was happening to Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not too far from an officer camp-- officer's camp. That's how my career in that camp ended. From an officer's camp, and they had radios. And they were passing the information to us. There were some of our people who had some kind of work to do in the officer's camp, and they would bring out that information. We would print that, write it out-- I don't remember, even-- and then take it to some of the forced labor. Now that information contained, also, the information that Jews are being killed. Of course, the extent of the-- of the Holocaust, we didn't know. We knew that there are concentration camps where Jews are being killed, but we didn't-- nobody knew the cond-- the extent of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the-- what was the year that you were in this camp? Is this--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, I would say '42, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And '44 beginning-- '44-- no, it was up till '44, up to January, '45 when, you know some-- in-- in this camp, anything of significance that happened that might be of interest is just-- the one Viennese guard who was-- who burst in one day with this happiness all over his face, the Americans are here. And that was June 6, 1944. You know, and this man was so anti-Nazi that I couldn't exceed him. You know, he was in charge of the-- of the horse-- horse barn where our horses were, you know. He was a guard there, and I remember him coming in to-- you know, the on his face was unbelievable. The Americans are here. And that was this camp. In this camp also, another story that happened once was that a truck got stuck in the mud, and I was with two friends-- Jews-- with me-- with me standing by. And the soldiers, the German soldiers who were driving the truck asked our help. Now French Jews-- and French altogether-- have a very special kind of thing. They do things, but they have to discuss them first for quite a long time, and talk about girls, and talk about food. You know, it takes time before they are ready to do anything. And, you know, they were talking. They were discussing what they supposed to do. And this German was very impatient and jump off and-- and pushed one of my friends. And I jumped him and pushed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyhow, that was separated. That was very fast finished, and the next day the guard comes to me and says, you're very lucky. I said, why am I lucky? Because this soldier started-- made a complaint to the Kommandant of the camp, who just came from the Russian front. And there was a big meeting, and at the meeting he said that a prisoner pushed me. And he said, what happened? So he told him this story, and he says, you know we're not supposed to push the other prisoners. You know, they are soldiers. He says, but they are Jews. Oh no, that doesn't exist. They are in uniform. They are prisoners of war. Don't push them. Very unusual.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is another thing that happened in that camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
